bins.name: AI Load Unique Characteristics
bins.desc: ATC describes AI data center loads as fundamentally different from prior large loads due to extreme second-to-second variability reaching hundreds of megawatts. This variability is unprecedented and requires new analysis tools and specialty power electronics such as STATCOMs.
quotes: AI load is different.  This is not just an expansion of the baseline data center loads that exist today.

we are familiar with proposals where the load seeks to ramp up and ramp down by hundreds of megawatts on a second-to-second basis.  This is equivalent to turning Wisconsin's capital city of Madison on and off, repeatedly.  These rapid, significant swings are unlike anything our industry has seen before and incomparable to any other load on today's grid.

bins.name: Targeted Reform Over Broad Rules
bins.desc: ATC consistently urges the Commission to pursue narrow, targeted changes rather than sweeping reform. ATC argues that overreaching rules risk disrupting proven local processes and could slow the interconnections the Commission seeks to accelerate.
quotes: the Commission should focus its efforts on targeted issues, rather than implement sweeping reform.

ATC respectfully requests that the Commission refrain from disturbing existing processes already in place to address the risk of speculative load and consider the risk of duplicating existing processes that could result in unintended consequences.

bins.name: Behavior-Based Applicability Threshold
bins.desc: ATC opposes the 20 MW size threshold as too broad, arguing it would capture ordinary non-data-center loads and overwhelm study resources. Instead, ATC recommends thresholds tied to operating behavior: ramp rate, active power oscillation, and low-voltage ride-through capability.
quotes: a 20 MW threshold would be too low and too vague for implementing load interconnection procedures.  A 20 MW threshold would include many existing and future non-data center loads.  This over-inclusivity would lead to unwieldy procedures and unintended consequences, including overwhelming study resources and likely delaying interconnecting the very loads that the Commission is seeking to expedite.
ATC recommends that the Commission consider adopting an applicability threshold based upon load behavior.

the Commission should consider adopting threshold applicability requirements based upon specific operating requirements for all loads in terms of ramp rates (i.e., how quickly they can increase or decrease electricity usage), active power oscillatory behavior (i.e., how much and how fast active power can swing once they are online), and ride-through capability (i.e., how well the load can remain connected and stable during grid disturbances such as voltage dips, frequency excursions, or short interruptions).

bins.name: Ramp, Oscillation, Ride-Through Criteria
bins.desc: ATC proposes that applicability turn on whether a load meets specific performance criteria covering ramp rate, oscillatory behavior, and ride-through capability. This approach targets genuinely problematic loads without imposing burdens on conventional customers.
quotes: ATC recommends that the Commission consider adopting an applicability threshold based upon load behavior.

the Commission should consider adopting threshold applicability requirements based upon specific operating requirements for all loads in terms of ramp rates (i.e., how quickly they can increase or decrease electricity usage), active power oscillatory behavior (i.e., how much and how fast active power can swing once they are online), and ride-through capability (i.e., how well the load can remain connected and stable during grid disturbances such as voltage dips, frequency excursions, or short interruptions).

the Commission should consider requiring new loads to bear 100% of the costs for facilities necessitated by their characteristics that do not conform with planning and interconnection criteria addressing behavior such as oscillation, ramp, and low-voltage ride-through.

bins.name: Hybrid Facility Rights and Process-Matching
bins.desc: ATC supports studying hybrid facilities based on requested injection and withdrawal rights in principle, but insists on process-matching requirements: the load and co-located generation must be operationally coupled so that generation failure also curtails the load. Without this, the transmission system becomes an uncompensated backstop.
quotes: ATC recommends that the Commission require 'process-matching' when considering how to study hybrid facilities.

If hybrid facilities are not process-matched and the load exceeds studied levels, the transmission system becomes the 'backstop,' and other customers subsidize that excess use through shared costs and reliability impacts.
strict operational limitations must be enforced, as unauthorized injections or withdrawals, particularly with oscillating data center loads, cannot be tolerated.

bins.name: Co-location Study and Process-Matching
bins.desc: ATC agrees that co-locating load and generation can reduce upgrade needs, but argues current MISO processes already coordinate joint siting. ATC's key condition is process-matching: if the co-located generation can fail independently, the full load must be studied and the load must bear the cost of full network service.
quotes: ATC recommends that the Commission require 'process-matching' when considering how to study hybrid facilities.

If hybrid facilities are not process-matched and the load exceeds studied levels, the transmission system becomes the 'backstop,' and other customers subsidize that excess use through shared costs and reliability impacts.

bins.name: Process-Matching for Co-located Facilities
bins.desc: ATC recommends a process-matching requirement drawn from its paper mill experience: the co-located generation and load must be operationally linked so that generation failure triggers an automatic load reduction. Without this link, other customers subsidize the excess demand through shared grid costs.
quotes: ATC recommends that the Commission require 'process-matching' when considering how to study hybrid facilities.

If hybrid facilities are not process-matched and the load exceeds studied levels, the transmission system becomes the 'backstop,' and other customers subsidize that excess use through shared costs and reliability impacts.

bins.name: Study Deposits and Withdrawal Penalties
bins.desc: ATC supports LGIP-style deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties for loads meeting the threshold, arguing these tools focus study resources on committed customers. ATC asks the Commission not to duplicate existing local safeguards such as ATC's Project Commitment Agreement.
quotes: ATC generally supports subjecting loads that meet the threshold applicability requirements to standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties, analogous to the LGIP.  Eliminating speculative requests for significant load growth from consideration by implementing these stringent requirements would help focus time, costs, and resources to expeditiously interconnect serious customers.

ATC respectfully requests that the Commission refrain from disturbing existing processes already in place to address the risk of speculative load and consider the risk of duplicating existing processes that could result in unintended consequences.

bins.name: System Protection and Operational Limits
bins.desc: ATC supports strict system protection facilities that can disconnect non-conforming loads and enforce injection and withdrawal limits. ATC backs NERC standards for large loads with oscillatory behavior and supports penalties for unauthorized injections or withdrawals, paired with transparency requirements.
quotes: strict operational limitations must be enforced, as unauthorized injections or withdrawals, particularly with oscillating data center loads, cannot be tolerated.

ATC strongly supports principles that uphold cost responsibility and maintain reliability, including proposed penalties for unauthorized injections or withdrawals.  However, any implementation plan must avoid unintended cost shifts and ensure transparency for transmission customers.

ATC strongly supports the implementation of robust operational standards for large load interconnections, particularly those exhibiting significant oscillatory behavior, because such behavior can cause voltage and frequency instability if not properly managed.

bins.name: Curtailability Under MISO Firm Service
bins.desc: ATC opposes the curtailability concept for expedited study because MISO treats all network load as firm service, meaning operators cannot selectively shed one customer's load ahead of another's. Introducing curtailable large load could force other firm customers to be shed first, creating compliance and fairness problems.
quotes: in ATC's experience, these large load customers require 24/7, uninterrupted, guaranteed, reliable service.  From ATC's perspective, these customers will have network service, and pursuant to MISO's Tariff, all network load is generally treated as firm service.  Introducing curtailment of network load complicates system management and could create scenarios where other customers' firm load is shed before the 'curtailable' large load.

bins.name: MISO Regional Context
bins.desc: ATC operates exclusively in the MISO footprint and notes that MISO's tariff already treats all network load as firm. ATC argues that MISO's existing stakeholder-driven planning process already coordinates joint siting of load and generation, limiting the need for new federal mandates in this region.
quotes: in ATC's experience, these large load customers require 24/7, uninterrupted, guaranteed, reliable service.  From ATC's perspective, these customers will have network service, and pursuant to MISO's Tariff, all network load is generally treated as firm service.  Introducing curtailment of network load complicates system management and could create scenarios where other customers' firm load is shed before the 'curtailable' large load.

bins.name: Study Timeline: 120 Days, Not 60
bins.desc: ATC opposes the 60-day study deadline, citing an actual 18-month study costing roughly $400,000 for a complex AI load. ATC recommends 120 days as a realistic minimum and asks the Commission to preserve transmission owner flexibility in scheduling studies.
quotes: in ATC's experience, these large load customers require 24/7, uninterrupted, guaranteed, reliable service.  From ATC's perspective, these customers will have network service, and pursuant to MISO's Tariff, all network load is generally treated as firm service.  Introducing curtailment of network load complicates system management and could create scenarios where other customers' firm load is shed before the 'curtailable' large load.
ATC recently completed an 18-month study costing approximately $400,000 in contracted engineering services, primarily due to the novelty and complexity of the work.
ATC recommends setting a more realistic expectation of 120 days for study completion.

bins.name: Study Complexity and Local Flexibility
bins.desc: ATC supports joint study of load and generation in principle but warns that combining new generation and new loads in shared models adds complexity and can make cost allocation extremely difficult. ATC urges the Commission to preserve transmission owner discretion to manage study schedules and model updates.
quotes: we are familiar with proposals where the load seeks to ramp up and ramp down by hundreds of megawatts on a second-to-second basis.  This is equivalent to turning Wisconsin's capital city of Madison on and off, repeatedly.  These rapid, significant swings are unlike anything our industry has seen before and incomparable to any other load on today's grid.

the Commission should consider adopting threshold applicability requirements based upon specific operating requirements for all loads in terms of ramp rates (i.e., how quickly they can increase or decrease electricity usage), active power oscillatory behavior (i.e., how much and how fast active power can swing once they are online), and ride-through capability (i.e., how well the load can remain connected and stable during grid disturbances such as voltage dips, frequency excursions, or short interruptions).
ATC recently completed an 18-month study costing approximately $400,000 in contracted engineering services, primarily due to the novelty and complexity of the work.
ATC recommends setting a more realistic expectation of 120 days for study completion.

bins.name: Network Upgrade Cost Allocation
bins.desc: ATC opposes blanket direct assignment of all network upgrade costs, arguing most upgrades benefit all customers and should be socialized. ATC supports direct assignment only for facilities exclusively attributable to the new load (analogous to generator TOIFs) and for specialty equipment required because the load fails to meet performance criteria.
quotes: ATC maintains that such costs should be socialized because these network upgrades ultimately benefit all customers.
ATC recommends that the Commission narrowly tailor direct assignment of interconnection facility costs to those that can be directly attributed to and likely exclusively used by the new load.

the Commission should consider requiring new loads to bear 100% of the costs for facilities necessitated by their characteristics that do not conform with planning and interconnection criteria addressing behavior such as oscillation, ramp, and low-voltage ride-through.

ATC encourages the Commission to incorporate transmission owner initial-funding in any future requirements related to load interconnection procedures.  Without the option to self-fund load interconnection projects, a customer's option to build would present several concerns to ATC.

bins.name: Cost Causation and Socialization Balance
bins.desc: ATC supports cost-causation principles and penalties for unauthorized usage but argues that network upgrades serving the shared grid should be socialized. ATC recommends directly assigning only the identifiable, exclusive-use facilities and non-conforming-behavior equipment costs.
quotes: If hybrid facilities are not process-matched and the load exceeds studied levels, the transmission system becomes the 'backstop,' and other customers subsidize that excess use through shared costs and reliability impacts.

ATC strongly supports principles that uphold cost responsibility and maintain reliability, including proposed penalties for unauthorized injections or withdrawals.  However, any implementation plan must avoid unintended cost shifts and ensure transparency for transmission customers.
ATC maintains that such costs should be socialized because these network upgrades ultimately benefit all customers.
ATC recommends that the Commission narrowly tailor direct assignment of interconnection facility costs to those that can be directly attributed to and likely exclusively used by the new load.

the Commission should consider requiring new loads to bear 100% of the costs for facilities necessitated by their characteristics that do not conform with planning and interconnection criteria addressing behavior such as oscillation, ramp, and low-voltage ride-through.

bins.name: Transmission Owner Initial-Funding
bins.desc: ATC asks the Commission to preserve the transmission owner initial-funding option in any future option-to-build framework so that ATC retains the ability to earn a return on and of assets it must own, operate, and maintain.
quotes: ATC encourages the Commission to incorporate transmission owner initial-funding in any future requirements related to load interconnection procedures.  Without the option to self-fund load interconnection projects, a customer's option to build would present several concerns to ATC.

bins.name: Realistic Study Duration
bins.desc: ATC draws on direct experience to show that complex AI load studies take 18 months or more and cost hundreds of thousands of dollars. The 60-day target is unrealistic; ATC proposes 120 days and asks that NERC develop standard load models to help compress future study durations.
quotes: ATC recently completed an 18-month study costing approximately $400,000 in contracted engineering services, primarily due to the novelty and complexity of the work.
ATC recommends setting a more realistic expectation of 120 days for study completion.

bins.name: NERC Standards for Oscillating Loads
bins.desc: ATC supports new NERC reliability standards and registration categories for large loads with pronounced oscillatory characteristics. ATC asks that any new standards be limited to loads meeting the behavior-based threshold so requirements remain targeted.
quotes: ATC strongly supports the implementation of robust operational standards for large load interconnections, particularly those exhibiting significant oscillatory behavior, because such behavior can cause voltage and frequency instability if not properly managed.

bins.name: Commission Jurisdiction Scope
bins.desc: ATC supports limiting FERC jurisdiction to direct interconnections to transmission facilities under the seven-factor test, consistent with existing law. ATC stresses that its Wisconsin charter restricts it to transmission-only service and that retail load interconnections flow through local distribution customers.
quotes: the Commission should focus its efforts on targeted issues, rather than implement sweeping reform.
